chapter_summary: After the churning of the ocean, the daityas and danavas seize their weapons and rush upon the gods. The mighty Vishnu, together with Nara, takes the nectar (amrita) from the danava-lords, and all the gods drink it. The danava Rahu, disguised in the form of a god, also drinks the nectar; Chandra (Moon) and Surya (Sun) report this to the gods, and as the nectar reaches Rahu's throat Vishnu (Bhagavan) severs his ornamented head with his discus (cakra). The mountain-like head falls and shakes the earth, and from this springs the eternal enmity by which Rahu's mouth swallows the Sun and Moon to this day (the origin of eclipses). Hari then abandons his peerless female form (Mohini) and routs the danavas. A terrible battle erupts near the salt ocean between gods and asuras: weapons fall in thousands, asuras are mangled and slain, heads are severed, and cries of woe rise to heaven. When the tumult is at its height, the gods Nara and Narayana enter the fight. Seeing Nara's divine bow, Vishnu calls to mind his discus Sudarshana, which descends blazing from the sky; Acyuta hurls it and it tears the daityas by thousands, drinking their blood like a fiend. The asuras hurl mountains and ascend the sky, but Nara cleaves the peaks with his golden-tipped arrows, and the afflicted asuras flee into the earth and the salt ocean. The gods, victorious, honor Mount Mandara and return it to its place; they secure the amrita, and Indra (Balabhid) with the immortals gives the treasure of nectar to the Crowned One (Kiritin) to guard.

relationships.rel: flee into earth and ocean from
honor and return to its place

story.title: The Beheading of Rahu and the Rout of the Asuras
story.arc: Immediately after the ocean is churned, the asuras try to seize the nectar by force, but Vishnu (with Nara) recovers it and distributes it to the gods. The infiltrator Rahu is exposed by Sun and Moon and beheaded mid-drink by Vishnu's discus, his immortal severed head becoming the eternal eclipse-demon. Vishnu sheds his Mohini guise and a cosmic battle erupts by the salt sea; the gods, armed and aided by Nara-Narayana and the descending Sudarshana discus, slaughter and scatter the asuras into earth and ocean. Victorious, the gods restore Mandara, secure the amrita, and entrust it to the Crowned One for safekeeping.
story.timeline_note: Follows directly from the churning of the ocean (samudra manthana); explains the origin of solar and lunar eclipses through Rahu's enmity. Set near the salt ocean at the foot of Mount Mandara.
story.comic_potential: Vivid cosmic-battle splash pages: the disguised Rahu sipping nectar only to be decapitated by the spinning discus; his giant mountain-like head crashing down and shaking the world; the blazing Sudarshana wheeling through the sky drinking blood like a fiend; asuras flinging whole tree-covered mountains that Nara shatters arrow by arrow.

teachings.teaching: Immortality unlawfully seized cannot be kept: Rahu steals a sip of the nectar in disguise, yet because he took it through deceit his head is severed the instant he is recognized — the immortality he stole is forfeited even as it touches his throat.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework the amrita is the immortal nectar (amrit) that drips and is drunk through inner yogic practice, not grasped by force or fraud. Rahu represents the ego that tries to steal the experience of deathlessness without true sadhana; exposed by the witnessing solar and lunar currents (Surya and Chandra nadis), the counterfeit attainment is cut off at the throat (the vissuddha center) by the discriminating wheel of awareness.

teachings.teaching: An unresolved enmity becomes a recurring cosmic pattern: because Rahu was wronged in the act of his transgression, his severed head perpetually swallows the Sun and Moon, so the conflict repeats endlessly as the eclipse.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the ceaseless devouring of Surya and Chandra by the headless Rahu as the ego's residual karmic momentum continuing to periodically eclipse the inner sun and moon (the ida and pingala / solar and lunar luminaries within), until it is finally consumed in higher realization.

teachings.teaching: Divine force is wielded only by thought, not by strain: when battle is fiercest Vishnu merely calls the Sudarshana to mind and it arrives blazing from the sky, then tears apart whole hosts of asuras at his will.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the discus summoned by mere intention is the awakened force of consciousness that, once kindled, acts of itself; the yogi does not labor against the lower tendencies (the asuras) but, established in awareness, lets the luminous wheel of higher cognition dissolve them.

teachings.teaching: After victory the hard-won nectar is not consumed carelessly but carefully secured and entrusted to a worthy guardian.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma stresses that the inner nectar attained through practice must be conserved and protected (the bound, crowned guardian within), not squandered; the gods placing the amrita under guard mirrors the disciplined preservation of the immortal experience.
